Additionally, 'range checks sufficient to prevent field-overflow attacks on the chosen representation' is a functional limitation whose sufficiency varies by representation — for Merkle set inclusion, what constitutes a 'field-overflow attack'? This invites a Nautilus indefiniteness rejection.

design_around.name: design_around
design_around.evidence: The Markush group forecloses the five most obvious design-arounds: swapping bitmasks for thresholds, sets, intervals, or compiled policies. A competitor cannot trivially escape by choosing a different permission encoding from the common toolkit. However, the group is closed and enumerated — a sixth representation escapes cleanly. Candidates: RSA accumulator-based permission sets, polynomial commitment-based permission vectors, attribute-based credential predicates (BBS+ range proofs), or lattice-based subset proofs. Each is a known cryptographic primitive not in the enumerated list.
design_around.critique: The broadening is real but bounded. A well-resourced competitor (say, a team familiar with accumulator cryptography) could implement RSA-accumulator-based permission subset enforcement in 1-2 weeks — outside the Markush group, clearly non-infringing. The 'compiled policy language' species is the broadest but also the most vulnerable to indefiniteness attack, so a competitor would challenge that species specifically while implementing their accumulator-based alternative.

scope.name: scope
scope.evidence: The claim covers the primary product (bitmask-based delegation) plus four alternative implementations that a product might evolve toward. Scalar thresholds cover tiered permission systems. Merkle sets cover role-based access with large permission spaces. Interval encoding covers financial limit delegation. The compiled policy species, if it survives 112, would cover future DSL-based approaches. This is meaningfully broader than the original single-species claim.
scope.critique: Good commercial breadth across current implementations, but the 112 vulnerability of the non-bitmask species means the effective enforceable scope may collapse back to bitmask-only if challenged. The scope score assumes the claim survives prosecution as written — if the examiner forces restriction to the bitmask species (which is the only one with spec support), scope drops to the original claim's level. The compiled-policy species is commercially valuable but legally fragile.

original_language: (ii) proves that a delegatee permission bitmask is a bitwise subset of the delegator permission bitmask with Num2Bits(64) range checks
claim_text: (ii) proves that a delegatee permission set is a subset of the delegator permission set by enforcing, within the zero-knowledge proof circuit, that every permission granted to the delegatee is also present in the delegator's permission set, wherein the permission sets are encoded in any circuit-compatible representation and the circuit includes range checks or membership checks sufficient to prevent the delegatee from obtaining permissions not held by the delegator
rationale: Claim 15 is identified as the narrowest, hardest-to-design-around independent claim, yet it still recites 'permission bitmask' and 'bitwise subset' with 'Num2Bits(64) range checks.' This revision lifts to a functional genus — subset enforcement in any circuit-compatible representation — while retaining the requirement that the circuit itself enforces the narrowing (not an off-chain check). This closes the design-around in the integrated authentication-plus-delegation claim without losing the ZK-circuit specificity that distinguishes over UCAN/Biscuit.
tradeoffs: Removes the concrete Num2Bits(64) anchor from Claim 15, slightly weakening its Alice Step 2 position. Claim 15A (the Alice safety-net dependent) still recites the full specific stack, so the pair remains defensible. An examiner may require further narrowing during prosecution.

alice_101.name: alice_101
alice_101.evidence: The revised claim replaces the concrete Num2Bits(64) bitwise-AND mechanism with purely functional language: 'enforcing... that every permission granted to the delegatee is also present in the delegator's permission set' using 'any circuit-compatible representation' with checks 'sufficient to prevent' over-delegation. Under Electric Power Group v. Alsthom, claiming a desired result (subset enforcement) without specifying the concrete mechanism is functional claiming at the exact point of novelty. The phrase 'within the zero-knowledge proof circuit' provides some machine-tie, but the CAFC has held that merely specifying a computational environment (here, a ZK circuit) while claiming the function generically is the cryptographic equivalent of 'apply it on a computer.' The original Num2Bits(64) + bitwise-AND was a specific transformation; this genus reduces to 'enforce subset somehow in a circuit.'
alice_101.critique: The claim fails Alice Step 2 because the only inventive concept — the specific bitmask encoding with Num2Bits decomposition and per-bit AND constraints — has been stripped out. What remains is the abstract idea of permission narrowing dressed in ZK-circuit vocabulary. The word 'sufficient' in 'checks sufficient to prevent' is a neon sign for functional claiming. An examiner will cite BSG Tech and Two-Way Media to reject.

obviousness_103.name: obviousness_103
obviousness_103.evidence: By broadening from bitmask+Num2Bits to 'any circuit-compatible representation,' the claim now reads on any ZK-based subset enforcement — making it easier to construct prior art combinations. UCAN teaches permission subset enforcement in delegation chains (cleartext). Tornado Cash Nova teaches chained Poseidon commitments with ZK proofs. DAC 2008/428 teaches private delegation with credential attenuation. The combination UCAN (subset logic) + Tornado (ZK commitment chaining) + any ZKP credential system (circuit-based verification) covers the genus. However, the specific integration of all three in a single circuit with identity-bound commitments requires at least 3-4 references with increasingly strained motivation-to-combine, keeping this above trivial.
obviousness_103.critique: The original Num2Bits(64) bitmask approach had a specific arity and decomposition strategy not directly taught by any single reference. The genus erases this specificity. An examiner can now argue: 'One of ordinary skill, knowing UCAN's subset attenuation and knowing Tornado's ZK commitment chaining, would find it obvious to enforce subset checks inside a ZK circuit rather than in cleartext.' The motivation is straightforward: privacy. Three references, clear motivation — KSR predictable-result rationale applies.

support_112.name: support_112
support_112.evidence: The specification discloses exactly ONE embodiment: 64-bit bitmask with Num2Bits decomposition, per-bit AND constraints, and cumulative bit encoding for hierarchical permissions. The revised claim covers 'any circuit-compatible representation' including set membership, Bloom filters, attribute-based encodings, Datalog-style policies compiled to R1CS — none of which are disclosed. The phrase 'membership checks' appears nowhere in the spec's circuit descriptions. The spec's worked example is exclusively bitmask-based.
support_112.critique: This is textbook LizardTech v. Earth Resource Mapping: the spec describes one species (bitmask + Num2Bits) but the claim covers the entire genus (any circuit-compatible subset enforcement). Under Ariad v. Eli Lilly, the spec must demonstrate possession of the full claimed scope. The inventor possessed one technique; the claim covers all techniques. An examiner will issue a 112(a) written description rejection and likely a 112(b) indefiniteness rejection for 'circuit-compatible representation' (what representations are NOT circuit-compatible?) and 'sufficient to prevent' (Nautilus — what is reasonably certain here?).

design_around.name: design_around
design_around.evidence: The genus closes the most obvious escape — swapping bitmask for BBS+ selective disclosure or set-membership accumulators. A competitor using any ZK circuit for permission subset enforcement would infringe. However, escapes remain: (1) perform subset enforcement in a TEE/MPC rather than a ZK circuit; (2) use a non-circuit ZKP system (e.g., MPC-in-the-head like Ligero, or lattice-based proofs that don't map to 'circuits' in the R1CS/PLONK sense); (3) enforce subset off-chain with a ZK proof only for identity, not permission narrowing; (4) use a different delegation model entirely (e.g., proxy re-encryption with policy attenuation). Each escape requires moderate architectural redesign (days of work) but is technically feasible.
design_around.critique: The broadening helps design-around resistance compared to the original bitmask-only claim, but the 112 vulnerability creates a paradox: the broader the claim, the weaker the 112 support, and any claim that survives prosecution will likely be narrowed back toward the bitmask embodiment anyway. The practical design-around resistance is moderate — the claim covers the ZK-circuit approach space well but leaves non-circuit privacy approaches open.

scope.name: scope
scope.evidence: The genus covers bitmask, set-membership, and any other circuit-based subset enforcement, capturing the primary product plus foreseeable ZK-circuit-based alternatives. This includes Circom/R1CS, PLONK/AIR, and Halo2 circuit architectures. The claim also implicitly covers future circuit compilation targets (e.g., Nova folding schemes that compile to R1CS). However, scope is limited to 'within the zero-knowledge proof circuit' — non-ZK and non-circuit approaches fall outside.
scope.critique: Good commercial breadth for ZK-circuit implementations, which is the dominant approach in 2026. But the 112 problem means the actual enforceable scope after prosecution will likely be narrower than what's claimed. The claim also doesn't reach TEE-based or MPC-based delegation, which are commercially viable alternatives that well-funded competitors (e.g., Mysten, Circle) might prefer.

original_language:
constrain bit-by-bit that the delegatee scope is a subset of the delegator scope;
enforce a cumulative bit encoding invariant on the delegatee scope;
claim_text:
constrain that the delegatee scope is a subset of the delegator scope by enforcing within the circuit that no permission present in the delegatee scope is absent from the delegator scope, using a permission narrowing mechanism that operates on the chosen permission encoding, wherein in a bitmask encoding the mechanism comprises per-bit multiplication constraints and in alternative encodings the mechanism comprises equivalent algebraic or set-membership constraints that enforce the subset property;
enforce, when the permission encoding defines a hierarchical tier structure, an invariant that a higher-tier permission implies the presence of all lower-tier permissions, the invariant being enforced by circuit constraints that prevent the delegatee from holding a higher-tier permission without also holding each implied lower-tier permission;
rationale: Claim 19 is the system claim describing the delegation circuit configuration. This revision broadens both the subset enforcement and the cumulative bit invariant simultaneously. The subset step now covers any encoding with an explicit bitmask species. The cumulative invariant is reframed from bit-specific language to a generic hierarchical tier implication rule, closing the attacker's observation that 'a single scalar permission level' trivially avoids the cumulative bit encoding. By conditioning the hierarchy invariant on 'when the permission encoding defines a hierarchical tier structure,' the claim avoids requiring the invariant for flat permission models while still covering it for any tiered scheme.
tradeoffs: The conditional 'when the permission encoding defines a hierarchical tier structure' means a competitor using a flat scalar threshold escapes the hierarchy invariant sub-element entirely — but that sub-element was already avoidable. The broader subset genus in the first part is the primary defensive improvement. The combined edit is longer, which may invite clarity objections.

alice_101.name: alice_101
alice_101.evidence: The claim recites 'per-bit multiplication constraints' and 'algebraic or set-membership constraints' but these are functional descriptions of what the circuit does, not specific circuit topology. The phrase 'equivalent algebraic or set-membership constraints that enforce the subset property' is pure result-oriented functional language — it claims the goal, not the mechanism. Compare Electric Power Group: claiming the result of 'enforcing the subset property' without specifying the concrete constraint wiring is abstract. The bitmask species recites 'per-bit multiplication constraints' which has some technical specificity, but the genus swallows it.
alice_101.critique: The genus language 'no permission present in the delegatee scope is absent from the delegator scope, using a permission narrowing mechanism that operates on the chosen permission encoding' is textbook functional claiming at the point of novelty. Under Two-Way Media, claiming a function ('enforce subset property') performed by unspecified means ('equivalent algebraic or set-membership constraints') is insufficient to transform the abstract idea. The bitmask species saves you from a 4, but the genus that actually matters for scope is firmly in functional-at-the-point-of-novelty territory.

obviousness_103.name: obviousness_103
obviousness_103.evidence: Subset enforcement in delegation is taught by UCAN and Biscuit (cleartext). Moving subset enforcement into a ZK circuit is taught by the combination of Tornado Cash Nova (chained Poseidon commitments in ZK) + UCAN (capability attenuation). The bitmask-specific per-bit multiplication constraint is a straightforward implementation of subset checking in arithmetic circuits — any competent circuit engineer would reach for bit decomposition + per-bit AND. The hierarchical tier implication is a design choice, not a non-obvious integration. However, the specific combination of scope-commitment-bound delegation chains with both subset enforcement AND hierarchical tier invariants within a single ZK circuit, tied to identity commitments, requires stitching together 3-4 references (UCAN + Tornado Nova + Semaphore + DAC-2008) with increasingly strained motivation.
obviousness_103.critique: A three-reference combination (UCAN for attenuation semantics, Tornado Nova for chained ZK commitments, Semaphore for identity Merkle proofs) gets you most of the way. The hierarchical tier invariant adds a fourth reference but it's a known pattern in RBAC systems. KSR's 'obvious to try' standard applies: given a known need for privacy-preserving delegation, combining known subset enforcement with known ZK commitment chaining is the predictable path. The specific integration pattern saves you from the 5-9 band but barely.

support_112.name: support_112
support_112.evidence: The specification discloses the bitmask embodiment in detail (Poseidon hashes, per-bit constraints, cumulative encoding). However, the genus 'alternative encodings... equivalent algebraic or set-membership constraints' has NO disclosed species beyond the bitmask. The spec does not work through any alternative encoding — no scalar threshold example, no set-membership example, no attribute-based example. Under LizardTech, claiming a genus with only one disclosed species is a written description failure. The conditional hierarchical tier language ('when the permission encoding defines a hierarchical tier structure') introduces scope ambiguity: what constitutes a 'hierarchical tier structure' is undefined.
support_112.critique: This is a classic LizardTech problem. You disclosed bitmasks. You now claim 'any encoding with equivalent constraints.' Where in the specification did you demonstrate possession of these alternative encodings? The phrase 'equivalent algebraic or set-membership constraints' is a functional genus anchored by exactly one species (bitmask multiplication). Under Ariad v. Eli Lilly, the spec must show the inventor possessed the full scope claimed. A single worked example cannot support a genus that purports to cover every possible permission encoding scheme. Additionally, 'hierarchical tier structure' lacks a clear definition — is a two-level admin/user split a 'tier structure'? Is a continuous scalar? Nautilus definiteness concerns.

design_around.name: design_around
design_around.evidence: The genus language attempts to close the bitmask escape, but the conditional hierarchy clause ('when the permission encoding defines a hierarchical tier structure') explicitly releases competitors using flat permission models. A competitor can use a simple scalar threshold permission system (permission level >= N) which is neither a bitmask nor a hierarchical tier structure as described, escaping both the subset genus (which requires 'no permission present in delegatee is absent from delegator' — inapplicable to scalar thresholds where permissions aren't discrete set members) and the hierarchy invariant. Alternatively, a competitor could use allowlist-based delegation (explicit enumeration of permitted actions per hop) rather than any encoding-based subset scheme.
design_around.critique: The genus broadening is aspirational but the 112 weakness undermines it. Even if the genus survives prosecution, the language 'no permission present in the delegatee scope is absent from the delegator scope' presupposes discrete, enumerable permissions. A competitor using continuous-valued permission bounds, probabilistic access control, or policy-language-based delegation (Datalog, Rego) sidesteps entirely. The escape cost is moderate — days to redesign the permission model — but the architectural change is localized to the delegation circuit, not the entire system. The conditional hierarchy clause is a free pass for flat models.

scope.name: scope
scope.evidence: The claim covers bitmask-based delegation (primary product) and attempts to cover alternative encodings via genus language. The hierarchical tier invariant covers the cumulative encoding scheme disclosed. The genus theoretically extends to set-membership and algebraic alternatives, giving coverage beyond the primary embodiment. However, the conditional hierarchy clause narrows scope for non-tiered permission models, and the 112 weakness means the genus may not survive prosecution.
scope.critique: If the genus survives (unlikely without additional species in the spec), scope is decent — it covers bitmask products and claims to cover alternatives. But a genus that fails 112 has zero effective scope beyond the bitmask species. Realistically, after prosecution you'll be left with the bitmask species plus maybe one narrow extension. The conditional hierarchy clause deliberately sacrifices scope for flat permission models, which is honest drafting but reduces commercial coverage. Future CIP candidates (recursive SNARKs, cross-chain delegation) are not addressed.

original_language: and (iii) writes the identity-bound scope commitment from the second zero-knowledge proof into a storage variable of the verifier contract indexed by the session nonce, the storage variable being subsequently read by a delegation verification function to enforce chain continuity; and
claim_text: and (iii) atomically writes the identity-bound scope commitment from the second zero-knowledge proof into a chain-state record of the verifier contract indexed by the session nonce, the chain-state record being a mapping writable exclusively by the verifier contract's handshake verification logic to seed an initial chain anchor and by the verifier contract's delegation verification logic to advance the chain by one hop, wherein the delegation verification logic reads the previous scope commitment exclusively from said chain-state record rather than from any caller-supplied parameter, such that every delegation chain is cryptographically rooted in the identity-bound scope commitment atomically seeded by the handshake of this step (d) and no delegation proof can be verified for the session nonce unless said chain-state record contains a scope commitment value written by a prior successful handshake verification; and
rationale: The attack correctly identifies that running two ZKP membership checks in one transaction is a predictable Aztec Connect pattern. The novel step is not the batch verification but the atomic chain-seeding: the handshake writes a scope commitment that becomes the exclusive, non-bypassable entry point for delegation chains. This revision elevates step (d)(iii) from an appendage into a structural requirement by specifying exclusive-write semantics and the mandatory handshake prerequisite for delegation, which is not taught by Semaphore, Iden3, or Aztec Connect individually or in combination.
tradeoffs: Narrows Claim 1 to require the exclusive-write chain-state record architecture, precluding embodiments where delegation chain anchors are supplied by callers or derived from off-chain state. Also precludes embodiments where delegation operates independently of a handshake.

alice_101.name: alice_101
alice_101.evidence: The claim recites concrete on-chain mechanics: atomic writes to a chain-state record, exclusive-write semantics enforced by contract logic, mapping indexed by session nonce, and a mandatory handshake prerequisite. These are specific data-structure operations tied to a verifier contract, not mere 'apply it on a computer' recitations. However, the core concept — 'write a value to storage, then read it back later as a prerequisite' — is a well-understood software pattern (initialization guard / anchor record). The claim dresses up what is fundamentally an access-control check on a storage slot. Under Electric Power Group, the 'exclusive-write' and 'atomically seeded' language describes results achieved, not a particular technical mechanism for achieving them. The ZKP elements are inherited from the parent claim rather than introduced here, which helps but means this sub-step is largely a smart-contract storage architecture claim.
alice_101.critique: A strong examiner would argue that 'a mapping writable exclusively by two designated functions' is conventional smart contract access control (OpenZeppelin's onlyOwner/onlyRole pattern). The 'atomically' language adds nothing beyond single-transaction execution which is inherent to EVM. The cryptographic rooting is a logical consequence of reading from the mapping rather than a separate technical innovation. Score held back from 15+ because no circuit constraints or cryptographic primitives are introduced in this specific step — it's contract-level plumbing.

obviousness_103.name: obviousness_103
obviousness_103.evidence: The revision targets the Semaphore + Aztec Connect combination attack effectively. No single reference teaches: (1) a scope commitment written by a handshake verification that (2) becomes the exclusive non-bypassable seed for delegation chains via (3) exclusive-write access control on the chain-state mapping. Semaphore has no delegation. Aztec Connect batches proofs but doesn't chain outputs into delegation anchors. UCAN/Biscuit attenuate capabilities but in cleartext with no on-chain anchor seeding. Tornado Nova chains commitments but for value transfer, not identity-bound scope delegation. The specific integration pattern — handshake-seeds-delegation-anchor with exclusive-write enforcement — requires combining at least 4 references (Semaphore + Aztec + UCAN + Tornado Nova) and the motivation to combine Tornado's chaining pattern with UCAN's attenuation in a ZK identity context is strained.
obviousness_103.critique: However, the concept of 'initialize a state variable in step A, require it in step B' is a ubiquitous smart contract pattern (e.g., Compound's market initialization, Uniswap's pool creation seeding LP state). An examiner could argue that once you decide to do both handshake and delegation on-chain (obvious combination of Semaphore + UCAN), linking them through a shared storage variable is a predictable design choice under KSR's 'finite identified predictable solutions' rationale. The exclusive-write semantics are just good access control hygiene, not a non-obvious integration. Score held at 12 rather than 15+ because the individual pieces (ZK membership proof, write result to storage, read storage in next step, access control on storage) are all individually well-known.

support_112.name: support_112
support_112.evidence: The provisional spec describes scope commitments, Poseidon hashing, delegation chains, and on-chain verification. The concept of writing handshake outputs to contract storage and reading them during delegation verification is inferable from the described architecture. The chain-state record indexed by session nonce aligns with the spec's description of session nonce enforcement.
support_112.critique: The specific language 'writable exclusively by the verifier contract's handshake verification logic... and by the verifier contract's delegation verification logic' introduces an access-control constraint that may not be explicitly disclosed in the provisional spec as a required architectural invariant. The spec likely describes what the functions do, not that the mapping must be exclusively writable by only those two code paths. Under Ariad v. Eli Lilly, the spec must show possession of the claimed invention, and the 'exclusive-write' limitation imposes a security architecture requirement that the spec may describe implicitly at best. Additionally, 'cryptographically rooted in the identity-bound scope commitment atomically seeded by the handshake' is a functional characterization whose full scope may exceed what the spec enables. The term 'chain-state record' is new vocabulary not anchored in the spec's terminology, risking an Interval Licensing definiteness challenge.

design_around.name: design_around
design_around.evidence: The exclusive-write requirement and mandatory handshake prerequisite create a structural constraint that competitors cannot trivially bypass while maintaining the same security guarantees. To escape, a competitor would need to either: (1) supply delegation anchors from off-chain state (explicitly excluded), (2) allow delegation without a prior handshake (excluded), or (3) use a different chain-state architecture entirely (e.g., event-log-based verification, off-chain state channels with on-chain settlement).
design_around.critique: The escape routes are moderate-cost but achievable. A competitor could: (A) Use an off-chain delegation tree with periodic on-chain root publication — delegation proofs reference the off-chain root, not a per-session chain-state record. This avoids the 'mapping writable exclusively by' limitation entirely. Cost: days of work. (B) Use a separate initialization transaction before the handshake that seeds the chain-state record through a different code path, breaking the 'exclusively by handshake verification logic' requirement. (C) Allow delegation to be rooted in a credential presentation proof rather than a mutual handshake, bypassing the mandatory handshake prerequisite. (D) The claim is narrow to EVM-style contract storage patterns — a competitor using a different execution environment (e.g., UTXO-based ZK rollup) may escape entirely because 'mapping writable exclusively by' has no direct analogue.

scope.name: scope
scope.evidence: The claim covers the primary product architecture where handshake verification atomically seeds delegation chains through an on-chain chain-state record with exclusive-write semantics. This matches the core IdentityOS product flow.
scope.critique: The revision deliberately narrows scope as acknowledged in the tradeoffs. It precludes: (1) embodiments where delegation operates independently of handshake (a commercially viable alternative for agent-only delegation chains), (2) off-chain delegation anchor derivation (likely the direction competitors would go for gas efficiency), (3) caller-supplied chain anchors (useful for cross-chain scenarios), (4) any architecture where delegation can be initiated without a prior mutual authentication event. This is a significant scope sacrifice. The claim essentially covers only the specific on-chain-storage-seeded-by-handshake architecture, leaving the broader concept of ZK delegation chains unprotected. A dependent claim covering this specific mechanism would have been appropriate; elevating it to independent claim language surrenders coverage of the genus.

original_language: and wherein the second zero-knowledge proof outputs as a public signal an identity-bound scope commitment computed as a Poseidon hash of the AI agent's permission bitmask and the AI agent's credential commitment;
claim_text: and wherein the second zero-knowledge proof outputs as a public signal an identity-bound scope commitment computed as a Poseidon hash of the AI agent's permission bitmask and the AI agent's credential commitment, the identity-bound scope commitment being atomically written by the handshake verification into the on-chain chain-state record of step (b) indexed by the session nonce to serve as the mandatory and sole anchor from which any subsequent delegation chain in step (c) must extend, such that the mutual authentication handshake and the delegation chain entry point are coupled through a single atomic on-chain state transition that binds the authenticated agent's identity and permission scope to all downstream delegation hops and that cannot be bypassed or substituted by any value supplied by an external caller to the verifier contract;
rationale: Claim 15 is the narrowest independent claim and the reviewer's recommended focus. This revision structurally fuses the handshake output to the delegation chain anchor within step (a) itself, so the chain-seeding is not merely recorded in step (b) but is described as an integral consequence of the handshake. This makes the atomic coupling between authentication and delegation entry a positive structural limitation that no combination of Semaphore + Iden3 + Aztec Connect teaches, because none of those systems produce an identity-bound output that gates a downstream delegation chain.
tradeoffs: Further narrows Claim 15's scope by requiring the chain-state anchor to be the sole entry point for delegation, foreclosing alternative architectures where delegation chains could begin from an independent enrollment step rather than a mutual handshake.

alice_101.name: alice_101
alice_101.evidence: The claim recites concrete cryptographic operations — Poseidon hash of permission bitmask and credential commitment, atomic on-chain state transition, session nonce indexing, chain-state record gating downstream delegation. These are specific technical steps tied to particular data structures (Merkle trees, bitmasks) and cryptographic primitives (Poseidon), not mere abstract-idea-plus-computer recitations.
alice_101.critique: However, the 'atomic coupling' and 'sole anchor' language is still largely functional — it describes a desired result (cannot be bypassed or substituted) rather than the specific contract-level mechanism that enforces it. An examiner under Electric Power Group would note that 'atomically written by the handshake verification into the on-chain chain-state record' describes what happens, not how the smart contract actually enforces atomicity. The claim would benefit from reciting the specific Solidity modifier, mapping structure, or require-check that prevents bypass. Still, the Poseidon hash with specific inputs and the chain-linking to downstream delegation hops provide enough technical particularity to survive Step 2A Prong Two as an improvement to blockchain-based authentication technology.

obviousness_103.name: obviousness_103
obviousness_103.evidence: No single reference teaches the combination of: (1) a mutual handshake producing an identity-bound scope commitment as a public signal from a ZK proof, (2) that commitment being atomically anchored on-chain, and (3) that anchor serving as the mandatory root for all downstream delegation chains. Semaphore teaches anonymous group membership but no scope commitments or delegation. UCAN/Biscuit teach capability attenuation but in cleartext without ZK. Tornado Cash Nova teaches chained Poseidon commitments but for value transfers, not identity-bound permission delegation. Combining these three requires at minimum Semaphore + UCAN + Tornado Nova + the atomic coupling insight.
obviousness_103.critique: A skilled examiner would argue the motivation to combine is not as strained as the applicant hopes. The idea of 'use the output of one ZK proof as the input anchor for a subsequent protocol step' is a known design pattern in rollup architectures (Aztec Connect batches proofs with shared context). The specific choice to make the scope commitment the 'sole anchor' is an obvious engineering choice once you decide to chain authentication to delegation — you need some binding value, and the scope commitment is the natural candidate. The 'atomic on-chain state transition' is standard smart contract design (single transaction = atomic by definition on EVM). The genuine novelty is narrower than the claim suggests: it's the specific combination of identity-bound Poseidon(bitmask, credential) as the chain root, which requires ~3-4 references to reconstruct.

support_112.name: support_112
support_112.evidence: The provisional specification describes Poseidon hashes of permission bitmasks and credential commitments, mutual handshake protocols, and delegation chains. The concept of the handshake output seeding the delegation chain is architecturally described.
support_112.critique: The claim introduces several concepts that need stronger spec support: (1) 'atomically written by the handshake verification into the on-chain chain-state record of step (b)' — the spec needs to show the specific smart contract code or pseudocode for this atomic write; (2) 'mandatory and sole anchor from which any subsequent delegation chain must extend' — the 'sole' limitation is a strong negative limitation under Santarus v. Par that requires explicit support showing why alternative entry points were considered and excluded; (3) 'cannot be bypassed or substituted by any value supplied by an external caller to the verifier contract' — this is a security property claim that needs explicit spec discussion of the access control mechanism. The provisional as excerpted describes the architecture at a high level but may not fully enable the 'sole anchor' and 'cannot be bypassed' limitations with the specificity Ariad v. Eli Lilly demands.

design_around.name: design_around
design_around.evidence: The claim's insistence on 'sole anchor' and atomic coupling between handshake and delegation makes it harder to design around within the exact architecture — a competitor cannot decouple authentication from delegation entry without leaving the claim scope.
design_around.critique: The design-around is moderate-difficulty but achievable in days, not weeks. A competitor can: (1) use a two-transaction pattern where the handshake writes to one contract and the delegation chain reads from a separate enrollment registry that is updated post-handshake (not 'atomically' in the same transaction), escaping the 'atomic state transition' limitation; (2) allow delegation chains to begin from either a handshake anchor OR an independent enrollment, escaping the 'sole anchor' requirement; (3) use a different commitment structure — e.g., hash(bitmask || credential || salt) with a different hash function or different input ordering — though this is weaker given the claim's Poseidon-agnostic structure at higher claim levels. The 'sole anchor' language actually helps competitors by giving them a clear escape: just provide an alternative delegation entry point.

scope.name: scope
scope.evidence: The claim covers the specific architecture where mutual authentication atomically seeds a delegation chain through a single on-chain state transition. This covers the primary product (IdentityOS mutual handshake → delegation flow).
scope.critique: The 'sole anchor' and atomic coupling requirements significantly narrow scope. Any product variant where: (a) delegation chains can be initiated independently of a mutual handshake, (b) the handshake and delegation setup occur in separate transactions, or (c) multiple entry points exist for delegation chains — all fall outside this claim. The applicant has traded scope for obviousness defense, which is a reasonable but costly tradeoff. The claim does not cover systems where agents can be pre-enrolled into delegation chains before a handshake occurs, which is a commercially likely architecture for enterprise deployments where agents are provisioned in advance. Future CIP candidates (recursive SNARKs, cross-chain delegation) are not covered because the atomic single-transaction requirement may not translate to cross-chain or batched-proof architectures.

original_language: (e) emitting a verification event only if both proofs are valid, the session nonce is fresh and matches both proofs, and the human user's nullifier has not been revoked.
claim_text: (e) emitting a verification event only if both proofs are valid, the session nonce is fresh and matches both proofs, and the human user's nullifier has not been revoked, wherein the writing of the identity-bound scope commitment in step (d)(iii) and the emission of the verification event in this step (e) are performed atomically within the single blockchain transaction of step (d) such that a successful handshake both authenticates the human-agent pair and seeds the chain-state record with the identity-bound scope commitment that constitutes the exclusive cryptographic entry point for any subsequent privacy-preserving delegation chain on the session nonce, and wherein a delegation verification function of the verifier contract reverts for the session nonce unless said chain-state record contains a scope commitment value previously written by the handshake verification of step (d), thereby coupling mutual authentication to delegation chain initialization as an indivisible on-chain state transition.
rationale: This revision attacks the obviousness argument from step (e) rather than (d)(iii), ensuring that even if an examiner reads (d)(iii) as a conventional write operation, the atomicity and coupling language in (e) independently establishes that the handshake is not merely two parallel membership proofs but an indivisible state transition that gates all downstream delegation. The 'indivisible on-chain state transition' framing distinguishes from Aztec Connect's batching (which batches proofs without seeding a downstream chain-state record) and from Semaphore (which has no delegation concept).
tradeoffs: Adds length and specificity to step (e), which was previously a clean terminal step. Creates some redundancy with the narrowed (d)(iii) if both candidates 01 and 03 are adopted simultaneously, though they can be applied to different claims in the family to create a claim-differentiation ladder.

alice_101.name: alice_101
alice_101.evidence: The claim recites concrete on-chain mechanics: atomic state transition coupling handshake verification to scope commitment writing, a delegation verification function that reverts unless chain-state contains a prior handshake-written value, and explicit session nonce matching across dual proofs. These are specific technical operations on a blockchain state machine, not abstract 'apply it on a computer' language.
alice_101.critique: However, the core concept remains 'verify two proofs and write a record atomically' — which an examiner could characterize as the abstract idea of 'conditional record-keeping upon authentication' performed on the generic blockchain computer. The atomicity constraint is a transactional guarantee inherent to any blockchain, not a novel machine transformation. The claim does not recite circuit-level constraints (Num2Bits, LessThan, specific Poseidon arity) that would push it into the 15-18 range. Under Electric Power Group, the 'indivisible on-chain state transition' framing is still result-oriented — it describes WHAT happens, not the specific cryptographic HOW at the constraint level.

obviousness_103.name: obviousness_103
obviousness_103.evidence: Combining Semaphore (dual Merkle membership proofs) + Aztec Connect (batched multi-proof verification with shared nonce) + UCAN/Biscuit (capability chain initialization) + Tornado Cash (on-chain commitment writing gating future ZK operations) requires at least four references. The specific integration pattern — handshake verification atomically seeding a chain-state record that gates downstream delegation — is not directly taught by any single reference.
obviousness_103.critique: The motivation to combine is strained but not absent. A PHOSITA building a delegation system atop anonymous credentials would naturally want the authentication step to initialize the delegation state. Tornado Cash Nova already chains Poseidon commitments where one ZK step gates the next. Aztec Connect batches proofs atomically. The 'exclusive cryptographic entry point' language is clever but the underlying pattern — 'write a commitment during verification that future operations must reference' — is Tornado's exact architecture. The examiner would argue: Semaphore membership proofs + Tornado-style commitment chaining + UCAN attenuation semantics = obvious combination with clear motivation (preventing unauthorized delegation chains). The 'revert unless chain-state contains prior handshake commitment' is a standard Solidity require-check pattern.

support_112.name: support_112
support_112.evidence: The provisional describes the handshake protocol, the scope commitment structure, and the delegation chain mechanism. The atomicity of the on-chain transaction is inherently described since both operations occur within a single smart contract function call. The 'delegation verification function reverts unless chain-state record exists' maps to a standard require() guard that would be described in the verifier contract specification.
support_112.critique: The specific phrase 'indivisible on-chain state transition' and 'exclusive cryptographic entry point' are prosecution arguments dressed as claim language — the spec likely does not use these exact terms. The claim introduces the concept that the scope commitment is the 'exclusive' entry point, but does the spec explicitly foreclose alternative delegation chain initialization paths? Under Ariad v. Eli Lilly, the spec must show possession of the full claimed scope. If the spec describes one embodiment where the handshake seeds delegation but does not explicitly state this is the ONLY path, the 'exclusive' limitation may lack written description support. The 'revert' behavior of the delegation function needs explicit spec support — is this described as a specific contract requirement or merely implied?

design_around.name: design_around
design_around.evidence: The claim ties mutual authentication to delegation chain initialization atomically on-chain, which is architecturally significant. A competitor cannot simply separate the two steps without fundamentally changing the security model. The 'exclusive cryptographic entry point' language means any system that allows delegation without a prior handshake commitment is outside the claim.
design_around.critique: The escape is straightforward: perform the handshake and delegation initialization as two separate transactions with a short time-lock or commit-reveal scheme. The handshake writes a temporary receipt; a second transaction reads that receipt and initializes the delegation chain. This is NOT atomic and NOT indivisible — it's two transactions — so it escapes the claim. Alternatively, use an off-chain relay that batches the operations but does not make them literally atomic in one transaction. A competitor could also initialize delegation chains through an alternative entry point (e.g., a governance-approved root delegation) that bypasses the handshake entirely, since the claim only covers the handshake-seeded path. Days of work, not weeks.

scope.name: scope
scope.evidence: The claim covers the primary product architecture — mutual authentication that seeds delegation chains. The atomicity requirement and the exclusive-entry-point language cover the core commercial embodiment and make it difficult to ship a functionally equivalent product without hitting the claim.
scope.critique: The claim is narrowed significantly by the atomicity and exclusivity requirements. Any system that performs authentication and delegation initialization as separate steps (even if closely coupled) is outside scope. Systems using off-chain delegation (L2 rollups, state channels) where the 'atomic on-chain state transition' doesn't apply are also outside scope. The claim covers the specific on-chain-atomic embodiment well but does not reach hybrid on-chain/off-chain architectures, which are increasingly the commercial norm for gas-cost reasons. The 'single blockchain transaction' limitation is particularly narrow — multi-transaction flows with the same security guarantees escape cleanly.
